claim: ruff S603 (subprocess untrusted input) at updater.py:595,598,600,967
rationale: All flagged subprocess.run() calls use list-form argv (never shell=True), with hardcoded command names (dbus-send, systemctl, bash) and locally-generated paths (tempfile, ENGINE resolved next to updater.py). No user-controlled input flows into any argv, so shell/command injection is not reachable. S603 fires on every subprocess call regardless of actual risk; safe for this desktop GUI.

claim: ruff S607 (partial executable path) at updater.py:629,652,653,656,663,967
rationale: Flags use of bare tool names (systemctl, dbus-send, bash) resolved via PATH. These are standard trusted system binaries for a Linux desktop app; using absolute paths would be non-portable across distros. No attacker-controlled PATH is in scope (single-user desktop session). Intended behaviour, not a lookalike-binary risk.

claim: shellcheck SC2001 at update_system.sh:267 (use ${//} instead of sed)
rationale: The sed is `echo "$dupe" | sed 's/^/      /'` which prefixes every line of a multi-line variable using the per-line ^ anchor. Bash ${var//search/replace} cannot match a line-start anchor across multiple lines, so sed is the correct tool here, not a stylistic slip. No change warranted.

claim: pyright/mypy (~32) "type[Qt] has no attribute AlignVCenter / QProcess.MergedChannels / bytes(QByteArray)"
rationale: These are PySide6 type-stub scoping gaps, not runtime bugs. Short-form enum access (Qt.AlignVCenter, Qt.PointingHandCursor, QProcess.MergedChannels) and bytes(QByteArray) are all valid and work at runtime in PySide6/Qt6; the bundled .pyi stubs just don't expose them at that scope. No pyproject/type-check config exists in the project, so these are not gating CI. Suppress unless the project adopts fully-scoped enum idioms project-wide.
